name: Projective Character Selection
core_claim: Projective character selection is not a single validated psychometric instrument but a method family: present the user with a small set of archetypal characters (images plus short descriptions) and ask which they would most want to follow / be like / spend time with, then infer latent traits from the choice. It borrows from two distinct traditions. (1) The classical projective-testing tradition (Rorschach, TAT) holds that responses to ambiguous stimuli reveal implicit motives and personality the respondent cannot or will not self-report; the modern, defensible descendant is the forced-choice IMAGE-based personality assessment, where choosing between images that prototypically express opposing trait poles yields a Big Five estimate. (2) The media-psychology tradition of wishful identification and perceived similarity, which shows that the characters people want to be like are systematically related to their own personality, attitudes and aspirations. The theoretical link to media preference is unusually direct and double-purposed: the same character-choice act that estimates a trait ALSO expresses a content/tone affinity, because characters are bundles of archetype, genre, tone and theme. So unlike a generic Big Five quiz (trait -> theme is a single inferential hop), character selection can short-circuit the chain: the chosen archetype maps to theme preference both via the inferred trait AND directly via the content the character connotes. The cost is interpretive ambiguity: a single character choice is overdetermined (aesthetic, aspirational, identificatory, escapist motives all mixed), so each item is a noisy multi-trait signal rather than a clean single-trait probe.
predictive_validity_for_media: Two evidence streams, neither giving a direct 'character pick predicts TV theme completion' effect size, but both supportive of the mechanism. (1) FORCED-CHOICE IMAGE ASSESSMENT validity is strong and the best direct analog: Kazim/Premuzic-style validation (MDPI Journal of Intelligence 2022, Study 2 N=431 via Prolific) of a 150-image-pair forced-choice Big Five tool reported convergent correlations with the questionnaire IPIP-NEO-120 of Openness r=0.71, Conscientiousness r=0.70, Extraversion r=0.78, Agreeableness r=0.60, Emotional Stability r=0.70 - i.e. image-based forced choice recovers self-report traits about as well as two self-report instruments agree with each other, and a machine-learning short form kept only 68 of 150 items (~2 minutes). This establishes that choosing between images that embody trait poles is a valid trait estimator; characters are richer image+description stimuli of the same kind. (2) WISHFUL IDENTIFICATION / SIMILARITY: Hoffner & Buchanan (2005, Media Psychology) found wishful identification is predicted by perceived attitudinal/personality similarity and by valued character attributes (success, intelligence for both genders; admired traits gender-patterned); Tukachinsky/Cohen and the Character Recognizability Scale work (Comm Research 2024) show deep-level (personality/attitude) similarity drives parasocial friendship and wishful identification more than surface attributes. This means the characters a person chooses to 'follow' are non-random with respect to their own traits and values - the choice carries trait signal. NET: the trait-estimation step is well validated (r~0.6-0.78 for image forced choice); the character->trait->theme and character->theme links are inferential, inheriting validity from the Big Five-genre literature (Openness <-> aesthetic/complex content the most robust, |r|~0.2-0.4). No published study validates predicting TV/anime THEME preference or completion rate directly from archetypal-character selection at onboarding; that is a product hypothesis, not an established effect.
instrument_design: The instrument is a short forced-choice (or pick-one-of-N) battery over character cards, each card = an archetype rendered as image + 1-2 line description, deliberately spanning opposing trait poles and tones. Design parameters that matter: (a) Cards per item - binary pairs minimise cognitive load and acquiescence; pick-1-of-4 increases information per screen but is harder to balance. (b) Archetype basis - a Jungian/Pearson 12-archetype scaffold (Sage, Caregiver, Rebel/Revolutionary, Lover, Jester, Warrior, Creator, Explorer/Seeker, Ruler, Innocent/Idealist, Magician, Everyman/Realist) is the common consumer framing, but for trait recovery the cards should be engineered to load on Big Five / sensation-seeking poles rather than on Jungian theory, which lacks psychometric backing. (c) Validated reference: the forced-choice image Big Five tool used 150 pairs full / 68 items ML-scored (~2 min), with a progress bar and selection sound effects (gamification). For HelpME2C's sub-2-minute, 6-10 item target, a minimum viable version is 6-8 forced-choice character pairs, each pair contrasting two archetypes engineered to separate the two most media-relevant dimensions (Openness and sensation-seeking/Extraversion), scored by a simple additive or small regression model into coarse trait bands rather than precise scores. Response format is behavioural-feeling (you pick a person, not rate a statement), completion time per item ~5-10s, total well under 2 minutes. Scoring should be calibrated on a holdout against a self-report Big Five short form before trusting it.
cold_start_applicability: Among the strongest of the personality methods for cold start, for three reasons. (1) Zero history required: the signal is generated by the choice act itself in the first session, unlike implicit behavioural inference which needs days of logs. (2) Dual signal: each pick yields both a trait estimate AND a direct content/tone affinity (the chosen archetype connotes genres and themes), so even if the trait inference is noisy, the raw archetype choice is itself a usable preference prior - you can map 'chose the brooding-detective card' to mystery-investigation/whodunit/moral-compromise without routing through a personality score at all. (3) Cross-medium portability: archetypes are explicitly medium-agnostic (the Sage, the Rebel, found-family ensemble exist in both TV and anime), so a character chosen in a TV-framed card legitimately informs anime theme inference and vice versa - this is a genuine advantage over lexical or genre-name elicitation which is medium- and language-bound. The caveat is overdetermination: a first pick conflates aspiration, aesthetics and current self-image, so signal quality per item is moderate, not high; reliability comes from aggregating 6-10 items. Realistically this is a high-engagement, moderate-precision cold-start primary signal whose biggest risk is interpretive ambiguity rather than unavailability.
group_compatibility_use: [uncertain] No located study applies projective/archetypal character selection specifically to two-person joint media recommendation. The extendability argument is structural: if each partner's character picks are scored to a trait vector (or, more robustly here, to a vector of archetype/theme affinities), the two profiles can be combined with the standard group-recommendation aggregators documented in the personality-aware group RS literature - averaging (and personality-weighted average / PwAvg, which weights members by traits such as agreeableness/openness, arXiv 2304.08851 and Quijano-Sanchez-style work), least-misery, most-pleasure, and intersection. For HelpME2C the more interesting property is that character selection produces an interpretable archetype/theme affinity vector directly, so two-person reconciliation can be done at the theme level (intersect the theme sets each partner's archetypes imply, then rank) rather than only at the abstract trait level - this is more legible to users ('you both leaned toward the rebel + the caregiver -> redemption-arc, found-family') and sidesteps the question of whether to average two personalities. Which aggregation predicts JOINT satisfaction is unestablished for this method; least-misery / intersection is the safer default for a two-person 'something we BOTH want' product (the North Star), with averaging risking a bland midpoint neither person chose. Treat the combination strategy as an explicit A/B hypothesis, and prefer aggregating the archetype/theme affinity vectors over aggregating inferred trait scores.
quiz_ux_implications: This is the method's strongest suit and the main reason to consider it: it is intrinsically disguised and gamified. The user picks a person they would want to follow - it reads as entertainment, not a survey, which is exactly the brief. Bias profile vs a Likert Big Five quiz: (1) Acquiescence bias is largely eliminated because there is no agree/disagree scale - forced choice between balanced options removes the yes-saying tendency (well documented for forced-choice formats generally). (2) Social-desirability bias is reduced relative to self-report (game/image-based assessments are repeatedly shown less prone to it) but NOT eliminated, and shifts form: instead of inflating 'I am organised', users pick the aspirationally flattering or coolest-looking character - so wishful identification can pull picks toward the admired/idealised archetype rather than the most self-similar one (Hoffner & Buchanan show wishful identification tracks valued attributes like success/intelligence). For trait estimation this is contamination; for a recommender it is arguably fine or even useful, because what you want to watch is partly aspirational. (3) Faking is harder than on a transparent quiz because the trait-relevance of each pick is opaque to the user. (4) Image quality and licensing matter enormously - cards must be visually appealing, tonally legible, demographically and culturally varied, and (critically) not depict real/identifiable copyrighted characters without rights. Format recommendation: binary forced-choice character pairs, 6-8 items, each with a short evocative description, framed as 'who would you rather follow?'; avoid making the personality purpose visible.
cross_cultural_validity: Mixed, with one clear weakness and one clear strength. WEAKNESS: the validated forced-choice image Big Five tool was English-speakers only (authors explicitly flag no cross-cultural validation), and Jungian/Pearson archetypes are asserted to be universal (collective unconscious) but that universality is theoretical, not psychometrically demonstrated across cultures; archetype salience, the connotations of a given character image (e.g. what a 'rebel' or 'lone warrior' signifies), gender-role expectations attached to characters, and which attributes are admired/aspired-to all vary by culture - and Hoffner-style wishful-identification patterns are themselves gendered and culturally inflected. So a card deck engineered and validated on Western users will mis-score non-Western users' traits. STRENGTH relative to other methods: character selection is far MORE portable than lexical/query-text methods (which are language-bound and break for Korean/Japanese/Chinese/Arabic morphology) because the stimulus is largely visual and the descriptions are short and translatable, and because narrative archetypes do recur cross-culturally even if their valence differs. For HelpME2C's multilingual catalog the practical implication is: descriptions must be professionally localised (not machine-translated), the character imagery must be culturally varied and not default-Western, and the SCORING (card -> theme/trait weights) should ideally be re-calibrated per locale, or at minimum the system should lean on the direct archetype->theme mapping (more robust) rather than the archetype->Western-trait-norm->theme mapping. Validate the deck against a translated Big Five short form in at least one non-Western locale before global rollout.
privacy_consent_implications: Moderate-to-high sensitivity, but more defensible than passive behavioural inference. Inferring a personality profile from character choices is still profiling under GDPR, and personality inferences can proxy Article 9 special-category data (mental-health, belief, sexuality correlates), so transparency obligations (Arts 13-14) apply and, if recommendations are solely-automated with significant effect, Art. 22. The mitigating factor versus implicit signals is consent legibility: the user performs a deliberate, visible action (picking characters), which is itself a clearer basis for informed consent than silently mining scroll speed - the user knows they are giving input, even if the trait-extraction purpose is disguised. That disguise is the tension: hiding the personality-profiling purpose to reduce faking is in friction with GDPR transparency, so the privacy notice must disclose that selections are used to infer preferences/traits even though the in-quiz framing is playful. Recommended posture, consistent with ADR-0012 and the project's stop-and-ask rules: store only coarse derived theme/affinity bands (and at most coarse trait bands), NOT a labelled clinical-style personality profile; do not surface an explicit personality label to the user; obtain specific opt-in if any inferred trait is stored; treat the raw picks as ordinary onboarding preference input (low-risk) and the derived personality inference as the sensitive layer to minimise or avoid. Re-identification risk is low if only coarse bands are kept. This method does NOT trigger the background-processing-without-user-action concern because the data comes from an explicit user action.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: This method maps to the theme vocabulary unusually directly because each archetype card can be authored to connote specific theme slugs, in ADDITION to its inferred-trait route. Archetype -> theme mappings (the direct, more robust channel): The Sage/Detective -> mystery-investigation, whodunit, ethical-dilemma, meditation-on-time, character-study. The Rebel/Revolutionary -> revolution-and-rebellion, vigilante-justice, power-corrupts, class-conflict, institutional-failure, satire-of-power. The Caregiver -> found-family, parent-child-rift, grief-and-loss, platonic-soulmates, redemption-arc. The Lover -> forbidden-love, unrequited-longing, toxic-relationship, coming-of-age. The Jester -> absurdist-comedy, dark-comedy, fish-out-of-water. The Warrior/Soldier -> war-and-its-toll, soldier-coming-home, survival-and-endurance, chase-and-escape, underdog-rise. The Explorer/Seeker -> first-contact, post-apocalyptic-survival, fish-out-of-water, finding-purpose, alternate-history. The Magician -> magic-system-cost, technology-runs-amok, magical-realism, nonlinear-storytelling. The Ruler/Antihero-in-power -> rise-and-fall, legacy-of-empire, corruption-and-cover-up, moral-compromise, complicity-with-evil. The Outcast/Loner -> loneliness-and-isolation, identity-crisis, trauma-survival, alter-ego, impostor-syndrome. The Tragic/Doomed figure -> guilt-and-atonement, vengeance-and-its-cost, existential-horror, meditation-on-death. The trait route (secondary): choosing complex/ambiguous/aesthetic archetypes -> high Openness -> nonlinear-storytelling, earned-ambiguity, slow-burn, magical-realism, cosmic-horror; choosing high-arousal/thrill archetypes -> sensation-seeking -> chase-and-escape, survival-and-endurance, folk-horror, existential-horror; choosing sociable ensemble-leader archetypes -> Extraversion -> ensemble-mosaic, heist-and-caper, found-family. The practical recommendation is to author each card with an explicit theme-affinity weight vector at design time (so the card->theme map is curated, not inferred) and use the trait inference only as a smoothing prior - this makes the relationship to the vocabulary the most direct and controllable of any method in this research set.
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE - as a leading candidate for the cold-start primary signal, with caveats. Rationale: it best satisfies the brief (entertainment-feeling, not a survey), has the lowest acquiescence/faking exposure of the self-report family, the trait-estimation mechanism is validated for image forced-choice (r~0.6-0.78 convergent), it is medium-agnostic (a real asset for cross-medium TV<->anime inference), and it maps to the theme vocabulary more directly than any other method because each card's theme weights can be authored by hand. Proposed shape: 6-8 binary forced-choice character cards (image + one-line description), framed 'who would you rather follow?', placed early in onboarding as the gamified hook, total under 2 minutes. Score each card directly to a theme-affinity vector (curated weights), and secondarily to coarse Big Five/sensation-seeking bands used only as a smoothing prior. For the two-person core case, aggregate at the THEME-affinity level (intersection / least-misery default, A/B against personality-weighted average) rather than averaging trait scores. Hard dependencies and flags: (1) Card imagery and copy must be original/licensed (no unlicensed real characters) and demographically/culturally varied - non-trivial design+legal cost, raise as stop-and-ask before building the deck. (2) Localise descriptions professionally and ideally re-calibrate scoring per locale before global rollout; do not ship the English-validated weights worldwide unchecked. (3) Privacy: store coarse derived theme bands, not a labelled personality profile; disclose the inference purpose in the notice despite the playful framing; treat any stored trait inference as the sensitive layer (ADR likely needed). (4) Calibrate the card->trait/theme scoring against a self-report Big Five short form on a pilot before trusting it - this is Complex-Logic-class work for packages/ml and warrants sub-agent-isolated tests. (5) Best combined with a light values/mood elicitation; character choice alone leans aspirational, so pairing it with a 'what are you in the mood for tonight' state question corrects the wishful-identification skew.
key_sources: Kazim, Premuzic et al. (2022), 'Measuring Personality through Images: Validating a Forced-Choice Image-Based Assessment of the Big Five Personality Traits', Journal of Intelligence 10(1):12, FREE/open access https://www.mdpi.com/2079-3200/10/1/12 and via PMC https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8883940/ (the key direct-analog validation: N=431, 150 image pairs, ~5 min / ~2 min short form, convergent r=0.60-0.78). Companion scoring paper: 'Scoring a forced-choice image-based assessment of personality: A comparison of machine learning, regression, and summative approaches', Acta Psychologica 2022, PubMed https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35780596/ , free VoR https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10152224/1/Kazim_Scoring%20a%20forced-choice%20image-based%20assessment%20of%20personality_VoR.pdf . Hoffner & Buchanan (2005), 'Young Adults' Wishful Identification With Television Characters: The Role of Perceived Similarity and Character Attributes', Media Psychology 7(4):325-351, ResearchGate https://www.researchgate.net/publication/247503440 . Tukachinsky & Cohen / 'Mediated relationships with TV characters: effects of perceived and actual similarity in personality traits', Studies in the Sociology of Language (Benjamins) https://benjamins.com/catalog/ssol.7.1.05coh . 'Beyond Perceived Similarity: Development and Validation of the Character Recognizability Scale (CRS)', Communication Research 2024, https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15205436.2024.2366224 . Open Psychometrics 'Statistical Which Character Personality Quiz' dataset (464 traits x ~2000 characters, ~72M ratings) - useful for empirically mapping character->trait profiles, https://openpsychometrics.org/ . Pearson 12-archetype framework (consumer scaffold; psychometrically weak) - background only. Projective testing overview (validity caveats): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Projective_test . Group aggregation: 'A Personality-Guided Preference Aggregator for Ephemeral Group Recommendation', arXiv 2304.08851 (free https://arxiv.org/pdf/2304.08851 ); 'A Survey on Personality-Aware Recommendation Systems', arXiv 2101.12153 (free). GDPR Art. 22 https://gdpr-info.eu/art-22-gdpr/ .
